---
title: "Project Proposal"
author:
  - Chia Jia Jin "Shawn"
  - TAN Wei Chien "Timothy"
  - ZENG Tianzhi "Tony"
bibliography: references.bib
notice: |
  @{ruiz2024designfrictionssocialmedia}
---

## Target Paper

@ruiz2024designfrictionssocialmedia investigates whether introducing design frictions into social media interfaces can reduce mindless scrolling without excessively harming user experience. The paper presents a controlled between-subjects study comparing a conventional infinite-scroll feed with a reaction-based feed that requires users to actively respond to each post before accessing subsequent content. Drawing on the normative dissociation framework, the authors operationalize mindless scrolling as impaired memory recall and show that participants using the reaction-based interface exhibit significantly better recognition memory for viewed posts, indicating reduced dissociation and increased attentiveness. However, this cognitive benefit comes with a trade-off: over half of participants report frustration, and only a small minority express willingness to adopt such an interface in everyday use. The paper’s core contribution lies in empirically demonstrating this attention–satisfaction tension and articulating design implications that emphasize contextual and optional deployment of friction-based interfaces, particularly for goal-oriented or educational use cases rather than general-purpose social media browsing.

### Architecture of Interactive Technology Described in Paper

The system is organized as a small set of modular components that together support controlled content presentation, interaction manipulation, and measurement of user behavior and cognition. At a high level, the architecture consists of a mobile application shell, interchangeable feed interaction mechanisms, and supporting measurement modules for memory, subjective experience, and behavioral logging. The major components of the interactive technology are outlined below.

- **Social media feed**: A vertical feed of posts sourced and adapted from Reddit, each containing an image and accompanying text. Post content and ordering are identical across both interface variants.
- **Reaction-based interface (intervention)**: A sequential interaction mechanism that requires users to select one of four reactions (“Like,” “Congratulations!”, “Inspiring!”, “Love it!”) or press a “Not interested” button before the next post is revealed.
- **Infinite-scroll interface (control)**: An unrestricted scrolling mechanism in which content is dynamically loaded as users scroll downward, with no interaction required to access subsequent posts.
- **Memory recognition test**: An old–new discrimination task administered after the browsing session, where participants classify posts as previously seen or new to derive hit rates and false alarm rates.
- **Survey instruments**: Five-point Likert-scale questionnaires assessing frustration, attention, motivation, and interface satisfaction.
- **Behavioral logging**: Instrumentation that records time-on-task and demographic information, including age, gender, and baseline social media usage.

## Implementation Scope

This project explores the design and implementation of *soft friction* mechanisms for content browsing interfaces, inspired by prior work on design frictions in social media but oriented toward practical, user-tolerable interaction design. Rather than replicating the reaction-based interface proposed by Ruiz et al., the goal is to identify a middle ground between unrestricted infinite scroll and compulsory per-item interaction—introducing gentle interruptions that encourage more intentional browsing without inducing excessive frustration or disengagement. Consistent with the findings of Ruiz et al., this project takes as given that introducing friction can improve attentiveness and recall; the focus therefore shifts from establishing cognitive benefit to identifying friction designs that remain acceptable and usable in everyday browsing contexts.

To this end, we will implement a friction-based browsing interface in which intentional friction is introduced intermittently and in varied forms. Rather than exhaustively implementing all possible friction mechanisms, the project will focus on **two or more representative friction modalities** that differ in interaction style and perceived intrusiveness. These friction mechanisms are designed to be lightweight, brief, and easily understandable, functioning as momentary pauses rather than hard barriers. The project explores multiple *friction modalities* to understand how different interaction textures affect perceived user satisfaction:

- **Interactive button or toggle**: A low-effort interaction where users press a button or toggle a switch to continue browsing, serving as a minimal motor-based interruption.
- **Content feedback elicitation**: A simple two-point rating that allows users to provide lightweight feedback on the content before proceeding, maintaining relevance to the browsing task.
- **Pause screen with quote**: A short, time-limited pause (5–10 seconds) displaying a neutral quote or prompt, intended to create a reflective break without requiring active input.
- **Mini-game interaction**: A brief, optional playful interaction (5–10 seconds) designed as a lighthearted pause rather than a cognitively demanding task.

In addition to modality, the *frequency* of friction introduction will be varied to further reduce disruption. Friction may be applied periodically (e.g., every 5, 10, or 15 posts) rather than on every item, allowing the interface to retain a largely fluid browsing experience while still introducing moments of interruption.

To approximate real-world browsing conditions and support ecological validity, the content feed will aggregate material from multiple online sources (e.g., blogs, newsletters, long-form posts), reflecting the heterogeneous nature of contemporary content consumption. Content diversity is treated as a feature rather than a controlled variable, as the project prioritizes user experience over experimental isolation.

Evaluation will focus on *user satisfaction and interaction behavior* rather than cognitive outcomes such as memory recall. As the primary objective is to design friction mechanisms that users are willing to tolerate in everyday use, the implementation emphasizes telemetry-based measures, including scrolling behavior, friction interaction events, dwell time, and session characteristics.

The final system will comprise the following components:

- **Content feed**: A scrollable feed aggregating varied online content.
- **Friction-based interface (intervention)**: A browsing interface that introduces soft friction mechanisms with configurable modalities and frequencies.
- **Infinite-scroll interface (control)**: A baseline interface with unrestricted scrolling and no friction, used for comparison.
- **Telemetry logging**: Instrumentation to capture scroll activity, friction interactions, content dwell time, and session metadata for subsequent analysis.

## Evaluation Methodology

The evaluation adopts a behaviour-driven approach aligned with the project’s design-oriented objectives. Whereas the target paper operationalizes mindless scrolling through post-consumption memory recall and self-reported experience, this project prioritizes *in-situ interaction behaviour* as the primary signal of user engagement and satisfaction. Building on prior evidence that friction can improve attentiveness and recall, the evaluation focuses on assessing whether softer and more intermittent friction designs remain acceptable during everyday browsing, rather than on further validating cognitive outcomes under controlled experimental conditions.

Content dwell time — the duration a user spends viewing each content item — is used as the primary behavioural metric. Dwell time is captured passively through telemetry and serves as an objective proxy for engagement quality and attentiveness. Compared to post-session surveys or recognition tasks, this measure reduces participant burden, avoids response bias, and more closely reflects naturalistic browsing behaviour in real-world interfaces.

The evaluation follows a between-subjects design comprising the following conditions:

- **Control group**: Participants interact with a standard infinite-scroll interface with no friction. Dwell time per content item is recorded to establish a baseline browsing pattern.
- **Intervention groups**: Participants interact with the friction-based interface. Each group is assigned a specific combination of friction modality and friction frequency. Dwell time per content item is recorded, excluding time spent on friction screens, to isolate attention directed toward the content itself.

Mean dwell time per content item is compared between the control group and each intervention group. An increase in dwell time relative to the control is interpreted as evidence that soft friction mechanisms encourage more deliberate and attentive consumption without fully interrupting the browsing flow. Conversely, a reduction in dwell time may indicate that the applied friction is perceived as disruptive or frustrating, leading to disengagement.

By relying on telemetry-based behavioural measures rather than post-consumption surveys or memory tests, the evaluation methodology reflects a deliberate shift from the target paper’s experimental framework toward practical interaction design assessment. This approach prioritizes feasibility, usability, and satisfaction in real-world use, complementing prior work that establishes the cognitive benefits of friction-based interfaces.
